meta.title: Window & Door Basics
meta.intro: A plain-English guide to how Florida windows and doors are built, installed, and measured — so you can spec the right unit, order it correctly, and explain it to a customer.

sections.label: Install Types & Frame Profiles
sections.blurb: How a window or door attaches to the wall — fins, flanges, and block frames — and when each is used.

topics.title: Nail Fin (Nailing Flange)
topics.summary: A nail fin (also called a nailing flange) is a thin lip that runs around the perimeter of the window or door frame. It laps flat onto the wall sheathing so you can fasten straight through the fin into the framing studs. After the unit is screwed off, you flash over the fin with tape, then run your siding on top to lock everything in. This is the standard install for New Construction on Stick Framed walls, and in Anchor it's the only application type that pulls nail-fin screws, which are priced per linear foot around the frame.
keyNumbers: Install type: Nail-fin
Best fit: New Construction, Stick Framed
Fastening: through the fin into studs
Pricing: nail-fin screws per linear foot
Weather seal: flashing tape laps over the fin
body: The fin sets the window plumb against the sheathing and gives a wide, even nailing surface, so the unit can't rack in the opening.
Flashing sequence matters: sill flashing first, then jambs, then head flashing laps OVER the fin so water sheds outward, never behind it.
Because the fin and flashing get buried under siding, this install is meant to be done before the exterior is finished, not as a swap-out later.
In Anchor, choosing Nail-fin is what adds the per-linear-foot nail-fin screw line to the estimate, billed around the full frame perimeter (head + sill + 2 jambs).
pros: Strongest, most weather-tight attachment for New Construction
Wide nailing surface keeps the unit square and rigid
Flashing tape over the fin gives a clean drainage path
Hides completely under siding for a finished look
cons: New-construction only — needs exposed sheathing, not for finished walls
Disturbs siding and flashing, so it's impractical as a replacement
Adds the per-linear-foot nail-fin screw cost to the job
Mistakes in flashing order can trap water behind the fin

topics.title: Florida Flange / Replacement
topics.summary: A Florida flange is a one-piece flange extruded as part of the window or door frame itself — not a separate nail fin clipped or screwed on. It's common on Florida impact units and usually comes pre-drilled for fasteners. It pairs with the Replacement (retrofit) install path: the unit drops into an existing opening and is anchored with a few screws per jamb instead of being nailed to the sheathing on new construction. Plan on a minimum of 3 #8 x 2" screws per jamb, more for larger or higher-pressure openings.
keyNumbers: Flange type: integral (one-piece, extruded with frame)
Install path: Florida Flange / Replacement (retrofit)
Min fasteners: 3 #8 x 2" screws per jamb (typical)
Best fit: existing opening, no fin to slip behind siding
Shim gap: window + 1/2" at the finished hole
body: Because the flange is part of the frame, there's no separate fin to crack, bend, or pull loose — it goes in as one rigid piece.
Pre-drilled holes set the fastener spacing for you; just confirm count and edge distance against the unit's Florida Product Approval / NOA.
Retrofit means you're setting into the existing buck or jamb, so the surrounding wall finish (stucco, siding, trim) usually stays put.
Always bed the flange in sealant and follow the approval's screw schedule — screw count climbs with size and design pressure.
pros: Fast retrofit — no need to tear off siding or stucco to tuck a fin behind it
One-piece flange is rigid and won't crack or peel away like a thin separate fin can
Pre-drilled holes speed layout and keep fastener spacing consistent
Ideal for remodel/replacement work in an existing opening
cons: Flange sits on the face of the wall, so it's visible and needs clean sealant/trim detailing
Replacement anchoring (screws per jamb) is only as strong as the existing buck — soft or rotted bucks must be repaired first
Less forgiving than new construction for getting the unit dead plumb, since you inherit the old opening
Must verify screw count/spacing against the unit's product approval — pre-drilled holes are a starting point, not always the full schedule

topics.title: Equal Leg vs Unequal Leg Frames
topics.summary: Equal Leg and Unequal Leg are both finless, block-frame profiles — the window has a side wall (a "leg") on each face instead of a nail-fin. On an Equal Leg the legs are the same depth on both faces, so the frame centers in the masonry opening. On an Unequal Leg the exterior leg is deeper, so it laps over the block or stucco face for a cleaner exterior line and a built-in caulk edge. Both are set on block/masonry and anchor THROUGH the frame with Tapcons (through-frame anchors) — the per-screw line item Anchor adds on every Equal Leg and Unequal Leg job, along with shims.
keyNumbers: Install types in Anchor: Equal Leg, Unequal Leg (both finless block frame)
Anchoring: through-frame anchors (Tapcons) driven through the frame into block
Equal Leg: same leg depth both faces — frame centers in the opening
Unequal Leg: deeper exterior leg laps the block/stucco face
Auto-added line items: through-frame anchors + shims (every Equal/Unequal Leg job)
Finished hole still = window + 1/2" shim space (e.g. 36x60 → 36.5x60.5)
body: Both profiles are finless — there is no nail-fin to screw to a stud, so they live on block/masonry openings, not in a stick-framed wall.

Because the anchor passes through the frame and into the block, the crew sets the unit, shims it plumb and square, then drives Tapcons through the jambs/head/sill — that is why Anchor bills these per screw, not per fin.
Equal Leg sits centered in the opening, so it reads the same inside and out and gives you symmetric reveals — handy when both faces are finished/visible.
Unequal Leg's deep exterior leg overlaps the block or stucco, hiding the rough edge of the opening and giving a natural surface to caulk against on the outside.
pros: Both: finless, so no fin to bend, break, or hide behind stucco — clean look on masonry
Both: through-frame Tapcons give a strong, direct anchor into block (great for Florida wind loads)
Equal Leg: symmetric reveals, looks identical inside and out
Unequal Leg: deep exterior leg laps the face — hides the opening edge and gives a built-in caulk line
Both: serviceable — anchors are reachable through the frame for inspection or replacement
cons: Both: anchors penetrate the frame, so every through-frame screw is a potential leak point that must be sealed
Both: only suited to block/masonry — not for a stick-framed wall (use a nail-fin there)
Equal Leg: no exterior lap, so the outside caulk joint does all the weather sealing
Unequal Leg: the deeper exterior leg must be sized to the block/stucco thickness or it won't lap cleanly
Both: more shimming and care to set plumb/square than a fin unit that self-locates against the sheathing

topics.title: Block Frame & Flush Fin
topics.summary: A block frame is a finless, square-tube frame that drops into a prepared opening or buck and anchors straight through the frame into the masonry. It's the retrofit and masonry workhorse — no fin, so it doesn't depend on stucco or sheathing to hold. A flush fin is a retrofit profile with a small fin that laps flat against the existing exterior face or old frame, letting you re-skin a tired opening without tearing off stucco. Pick block frame when you're anchoring into a buck or block opening; pick flush fin when you're covering an existing frame and want to leave the wall finish intact.
keyNumbers: Block frame anchors: through the frame into masonry/buck
Flush fin lap: small fin sits flush on the existing exterior face
Shim gap: window + 1/2" inside the finished hole
Stucco removal: none required for flush fin
body: Block frame = finless tube. It seats in a Block Framed opening or wood buck and is fastened through the frame jambs/head, so the wall finish doesn't carry the window.
Flush fin = block frame with a small applied fin. The fin laps the old exterior frame or stucco face, hiding the seam and weatherproofing the perimeter without demo.
Flush fin is a Florida Flange / Replacement style move: the old frame stays in place and the new unit nests over it — fast turnaround, minimal patching.
Block frame is the choice for New Construction into block or any Remodel where you've built a clean buck to anchor into.
Either way the finished hole still follows the rule: window + 1/2" shim space. Get the buck and masonry opening right first (e.g. 36x60 window → 39.5x63.5 masonry opening → 36.5x60.5 finished hole).
pros: Block frame: anchors through the frame — strong, code-friendly hold in masonry/buck
Block frame: no fin means it fits prepared openings cleanly with no stucco dependency
Flush fin: re-skins a worn opening without removing stucco or the old frame
Flush fin: fast, low-mess retrofit — minimal patching and finish work
cons: Block frame: needs a properly prepped opening/buck before it can go in
Block frame: anchors pass through the frame, so flashing and sealing the perimeter is on you
Flush fin: the old frame stays, so you lose a little glass area / daylight opening
Flush fin: only works when the existing frame is sound — rot or rust means full replacement

topics.title: New construction vs. replacement
topics.summary: New construction means the window goes in while the wall is still open — full framing access, and the unit is held by a fin (Nail-Fin on Stick Framed, or a Florida Flange / leg on Block Framed). Replacement (retrofit) drops a new frame into the existing opening of a finished house, so you favor finless block frames, flush fins, or a Florida Flange / Replacement unit. This single choice swings labor more than anything else: roughly 10 min/LF for stick-framed new work versus about 40 min/LF on a remodel. Block Framed adds its own load on top — wood bucking, block sealer, and concrete (Tapcon) anchors.
keyNumbers: New construction (Stick Framed): ~10 min/LF labor
Remodel / retrofit: ~40 min/LF labor
Labor swing: roughly 4x more time on a remodel
Block Framed adds: wood buck + block sealer + concrete anchors
New-work fin: Nail-Fin (stick) or Florida Flange / leg (block)
Retrofit favorite: Florida Flange / Replacement frame
body: New Construction: set before or with the wall finish, so the crew has full framing access and the fin fastens flat to studs or block before paper, stucco, or drywall closes it in.

Remodel: you work from the existing finished opening, so you protect interior and exterior surfaces, fit a frame that hides in the old hole, and seal back to finished stucco or trim — that's where the extra minutes go.
Application type follows the wall: Nail-Fin and Equal Leg / Unequal Leg fins suit open new work; Florida Flange / Replacement and finless block frames suit retrofits.
Block Framed on either path means anchoring into concrete with Tapcons, sealing the block, and (where needed) building a wood buck — material and labor a Stick Framed job skips.

Same opening math both ways: from a nominal size add 3.5" (or +3" from a labeled R.O.) to get the masonry opening the buck frames, then the finished hole is the window + 1/2" shim — e.g. a 36x60 window → 39.5x63.5 masonry opening → 36.5x60.5 finished hole.
pros: New construction is faster per foot (~10 min/LF) and lets the fin lap into the weather barrier for a cleaner water seal.
Replacement avoids tearing into finished walls — less patching of stucco, drywall, and trim for the homeowner.
Florida Flange / Replacement and finless block frames are built to slip into an existing opening, so the visible footprint stays small.
cons: Replacement runs ~40 min/LF — about 4x the labor of new stick work — because of surface protection, fitting, and sealing back to finished walls.
New construction only works while the wall is open, so it's not an option on a finished house.
Block Framed (either path) adds wood bucking, block sealer, and concrete anchors — more material, more time, and Tapcon drilling into masonry.

sections.label: How Windows Are Measured
sections.blurb: Nominal, rough opening, masonry opening, daylight opening, tip-to-tip — what each means and how to order without getting burned.

topics.title: The five measurements: Nominal, R.O., M.O., DLO, tip-to-tip
topics.summary: Five numbers describe one window, and mixing them up is how openings come out wrong. From smallest to largest they nest like this: DLO (the glass you actually see) sits inside the nominal/tip-to-tip frame, which drops into the R.O. (the framed hole, window + about 1/4" shim each side), which sits inside the M.O. (the masonry/block hole the buck boards frame). Get the call size straight, add the right amount for the opening, and every other number falls into place.
keyNumbers: Nominal / call size: a "3060" = 36 x 60 (catalog size)
R.O. = window + ~1/4" shim each side = W+1/2", H+1/2"
M.O. from nominal: +3.5" · from R.O.: +3" · from M.O.: as-is
Finished hole: window + 1/2" total shim (36x60 → 36.5 x 60.5)
36x60 example: 39.5 x 63.5 masonry opening
Size order: DLO < nominal/tip-to-tip < R.O. < M.O.
body: NOMINAL / call size — the catalog number you order by. "3060" means 36" wide x 60" tall. It is roughly the frame size, not the hole.
R.O. (rough opening) — the framed hole the window drops into: window + about 1/4" shim each side (W+1/2", H+1/2"). On Stick Framed openings the window goes straight into this R.O. with no buck.

M.O. (masonry opening) — the block hole the buck boards frame in Block Framed work. From a nominal size add 3.5"; from a labeled R.O. add 3"; a labeled M.O. is used as-is. All three converge on the same number.
DLO (daylight opening) — the glass you actually see through, measured inside the frame. It is the smallest of the five.

TIP-TO-TIP / overall frame — outside-to-outside of the frame including any nail-fin or leg. This is what has to physically fit, so it matters most on Florida Flange / Replacement and Unequal Leg installs.
pros: One window, five numbers — knowing which is which stops the most common ordering and cut-list mistakes
The relationship is fixed: DLO < nominal/tip-to-tip < R.O. < M.O., so any one number checks the others
Same conversions the calculator uses: nominal +3.5", R.O. +3", M.O. as-is all land on the same masonry opening
cons: Suppliers and schedules don't always label which number they gave you — when in doubt, confirm whether it's Nominal, R.O., or M.O. before cutting
Tip-to-tip can exceed the nominal size when a fin or leg is included, so on Florida Flange / Replacement and Unequal Leg units check the overall frame against the hole

topics.title: Sizing the buck: +3.5 / +3 / as-is
topics.summary: The buck frames the masonry opening, so you have to grow the window size out to that opening before you cut. Anchor handles three label types so you always land in the same place: from a NOMINAL size add 3.5", from a labeled R.O. add 3.0", and a labeled M.O. you use as-is. All three converge on the same masonry opening, and the finished hole always comes back to window + 1/2" shim. Example: a 36x60 nominal window grows to a 39.5x63.5 masonry opening, and after the buck and shim you have a 36.5x60.5 finished hole.
keyNumbers: From NOMINAL: add 3.5" each dimension
From a labeled R.O.: add 3.0"
A labeled M.O.: use as-is
Buck thickness: 1.5" per side
Shim gap: 0.25" per side (1/2" total)
Example 36x60 → 39.5x63.5 M.O. → 36.5x60.5 finished hole
body: Why +3.5 from nominal: each side gets 1.5" of buck plus 0.25" of shim, so 1.5 + 0.25 = 1.75" per side, times two sides = 3.5" added to width and to height.
Why only +3.0 from a labeled R.O.: a rough opening already bakes in the 1/2" shim allowance, so you only add the two 1.5" bucks (3.0" total).
Why M.O. is as-is: a masonry opening is the hole the buck frames, so there is nothing left to add — Anchor uses the number straight.
They all converge: nominal+3.5 = (nominal+0.5 R.O.)+3 = the labeled M.O. — three labels, one masonry opening.
The finished hole is always window + 1/2" total (1/4" shim each side), which is what lets you plumb and square the unit. For 36x60 that is 36.5 x 60.5.
pros: One rule per label type — no guessing which allowance is already included
Self-checking: if the three label paths don't land on the same M.O., a measurement is wrong
Leaves the correct 1/4" shim space per side to plumb and square the unit
cons: Mislabeling the input (calling an R.O. a nominal, or vice versa) throws the opening off by 1/2"
Assumes a standard 1.5" buck per side — non-standard buck stock needs the allowance adjusted

topics.title: Cross-manufacturer ordering gotchas
topics.summary: The same-looking call-out number doesn't mean the same thing across brands. One manufacturer's "3060" might be the unit/nominal size, while another's "3060" is the actual rough opening — and impact-rated lines often quote dimensions tip-to-tip (frame edge to frame edge), not the glass or the hole. Before you order or buck anything, confirm WHICH dimension the schedule is giving you, because guessing wrong puts you off by inches. That's exactly why Anchor makes you tag each opening as nominal, R.O., or M.O.: a single mis-tag throws the buck off by about 3 inches.
keyNumbers: Nominal to masonry opening: add 3.5"
Labeled R.O. to masonry opening: add 3.0"
M.O. label: used as-is, no adjustment
Mis-tag error on the buck: ~3"
Finished hole: window + 1/2" shim
Example: 36x60 window to 39.5x63.5 masonry opening
body: A call-out like "3060" is shorthand (roughly 3'0" x 6'0"), but shorthand for WHAT differs by brand — it can be the nominal unit size or the rough opening.
Impact / Florida-rated product schedules frequently list tip-to-tip frame dimensions, which are not the same as the hole you need to frame.
The fix is dirt simple: read the column header and the fine print on the schedule, then tag the opening in Anchor before bucking.
Because nominal+3.5" = (nominal+0.5")+3" = M.O., a correctly tagged opening always lands on the same masonry opening no matter which basis the brand published.
pros: Tagging nominal/R.O./M.O. makes every brand's schedule converge on one correct masonry opening
Catches the tip-to-tip vs. rough-opening trap before material is cut
One quick label check prevents re-ordering glass and re-bucking openings
cons: Requires reading the actual schedule header/fine print, not just trusting the call-out
Mixing brands on one job means confirming the basis for each line separately
A single mis-tag silently mis-sizes the buck by ~3" with no warning

sections.label: Product Types
sections.blurb: Windows, sliding glass doors, swing & French doors, and bifold walls — the styles, measurements, and install quirks of each.

topics.title: Windows: hung & casement
topics.summary: A single hung window opens by sliding the bottom sash up; the top stays fixed. A double hung lets both sashes move. A casement has no sliding sash at all — it cranks outward on a side hinge for full top-to-bottom airflow. All three are quoted by their nominal (call) size and can be ordered in any fin or leg profile to match the install. On the job they're bucked the same way: a 4-piece opening — head, sill, and two jambs — with the jambs cut 3 inches short so the head and sill run full width.
keyNumbers: Buck pieces (window): head + sill + 2 jambs = 4
Jamb cut: −3 in (head and sill run full width)
Sized by: nominal / call size
Finished hole: window + 1/2 in shim space
body: Single hung is the most common quote in Florida — only the bottom sash moves, so it's simpler and usually the lowest-cost option.
Double hung adds a movable top sash; both tilt or slide, which helps cleaning on upper floors but costs more.
Casement cranks out from a side hinge — best airflow of the three and a tight seal when latched, but it needs swing clearance outside.
The meeting rail is where the two sashes overlap on a hung window; on a casement there's no meeting rail because it's a single sash.
Order any profile: Nail-fin, Equal Leg, Unequal Leg, or Florida Flange / Replacement — the window choice and the fin/leg choice are separate decisions.
All three buck identically: head + sill + 2 jambs, jambs −3 in. (Sliding glass doors and bifolds differ — they drop the sill and cut jambs −1.5 in.)
pros: Single hung: lowest cost, fewest moving parts, no outside swing clearance needed
Double hung: both sashes operate for easy cleaning and balanced ventilation
Casement: best airflow and tightest seal when latched
All three quote by nominal size and accept any fin or leg profile
cons: Single hung: only the bottom half opens
Double hung: more cost and hardware than single hung
Casement: needs clear swing space outside and can catch wind when open
Casement crank hardware is one more thing that can wear or fail

topics.title: Sliding glass doors
topics.summary: A sliding glass door is a multi-panel unit where one or more panels roll on a bottom track while others stay fixed. Sizing is driven by the overall width and the panel count, with each panel marked O (fixed) or X (operating) — for example a 3-panel OXO has a center panel that slides past two fixed ends. These are heavy, labor-intensive units: budget roughly 4 crew-hours per panel plus a separate screw-inspection return trip before the trim covers go on. Because the work scales with panels, sliders are priced per panel, not per linear foot.
keyNumbers: Labor: ~4 crew-hours per panel
Pricing: per panel (not per LF)
Buck pieces: 3 (head + 2 jambs, NO sill)
Jamb cut: each jamb −1.5" (door rule)
Extra trip: 1 screw inspection before covers go on
Config example: 3-panel OXO = 2 fixed + 1 operating
body: Configuration is written in O/X notation: O = fixed (stationary) panel, X = operating (sliding) panel. Common layouts are OX, XO, OXO and OXXO — read left to right as you face the door.

The bottom track IS the sill, so the buck is only 3 pieces: a head and 2 jambs. Sliding glass doors and bifolds follow the door rule — jambs are cut −1.5" (windows are −3" because they also get a sill).
Plan the screw-inspection trip into the schedule: anchors must be seen and signed off before the head/jamb covers are installed, or you'll be pulling covers back off later.
Masonry-opening math is the same as any unit: from a nominal size add 3.5", from a labeled R.O. add 3.0", and a labeled M.O. is used as-is; the finished hole is the door plus 1/2" shim space.
pros: Priced per panel, so a wider unit with the same panel count doesn't blow up the quote like LF pricing would
Only 3 buck pieces (no sill) — fewer cuts and the track does double duty as the sill
O/X notation makes the operating layout unambiguous on the order and on site
cons: Heavy labor: ~4 crew-hours per panel adds up fast on 3- and 4-panel units
Requires a dedicated screw-inspection return trip before covers — easy to forget when scheduling
Glass and frames are heavy and awkward; setting and shimming the track level is critical for smooth operation

topics.title: Swing / French doors
topics.summary: Swing doors are hinged door leaves that pivot open on the jamb. A single-leaf is one slab; a French (double-leaf) door is two leaves that meet in the middle at an astragal. Anchor prices these PER DOOR, not per linear foot, with the threshold hardware already rolled into the per-door cost and a sill-flashing toggle for the peel-and-stick membrane under the sill. Plan on about 75 minutes of labor for a single-leaf and 105 minutes for a double; a single-leaf 36" slab also counts as roughly 3 LF when it feeds the rest of your material math.
keyNumbers: Single-leaf labor: ~75 min
Double-leaf (French) labor: ~105 min
Single-leaf 36in slab counts as ~3 LF
French double-leaf counts as ~6 LF (two 36in slabs)
Pricing: per door (threshold hardware included)
Sill flashing: toggle on/off per door, priced per LF of door width
body: Sized by the slab/leaf width, not by the opening — a 36in slab is the typical single-leaf; a French pair is usually two 36in leaves.
On a French door the two leaves close against an astragal — the vertical strip where the leaves meet and seal — while a single-leaf just latches into the strike jamb.
Threshold hardware (sill, sweep, anchors) is always included in the per-door price, so you don't line-item it separately.
Turn sill flashing on for new construction setting a door over concrete; turn it off when flashing is supplied separately or reused on a remodel.
pros: Per-door pricing is simple to quote — no LF math to build the door price itself
Threshold hardware is bundled in, so nothing gets forgotten on the line item
French/double-leaf gives a wide clear opening without the cost of a slider or bifold
cons: A French door's astragal and second leaf add hardware and alignment time — hence ~105 min vs ~75 min
Sill flashing is an easy toggle to overlook; leaving it off over concrete invites water intrusion
Hinged leaves need clear floor space for the swing arc, unlike a slider

topics.title: Bifold / Folding Glass Walls
topics.summary: A bifold (folding glass wall) is a run of hinged glass leaves that fold against each other and stack to one or both sides, opening up a whole wall instead of just a doorway. You size it by the overall opening width and the number of leaves — figure roughly 32" per leaf as a typical starting point. It's the most labor-intensive opening Anchor quotes (plan on about 5 crew-hours per leaf, plus a return trip for screw inspection), so it's priced per panel/leaf rather than as a single unit.
keyNumbers: Leaf width: ~32" per leaf (typical)
Labor: ~5 crew-hours per leaf
Buck pieces: 3 (head + 2 jambs, no sill)
Jamb cut: head width minus 1.5"
Extra trip: 1 screw-inspection visit
Pricing basis: per panel / per leaf
body: Stacking direction is a design choice: leaves can fold to one side or split and stack both ways — confirm it before ordering, since it drives the hinge layout and where the stack lands.
The top track carries the weight of the leaves on its rollers, so the head must be set dead-level and solid — this is what makes bifolds buck as 3 pieces instead of 4.
Like sliding glass doors, a folding wall is bucked head + 2 jambs with NO sill, and the jambs are cut to head width minus 1.5".

Standard buck math still applies to the opening: a nominal size gets +3.5", a labeled R.O. gets +3.0", and a labeled M.O. is used as-is to find the masonry opening the buck frames; the finished hole is the unit width plus 1/2" shim.
The screw-inspection trip is a separate scheduled visit, not the same day as install — build it into the timeline and the price.
pros: Opens an entire wall, not just a doorway — biggest indoor/outdoor connection of any option
Flexible stacking: fold to one side or both
Priced per leaf, so the quote scales cleanly with opening size
cons: Most labor-intensive opening Anchor installs (~5 crew-hours per leaf)
Requires an extra screw-inspection trip, adding time and cost
Top-track/roller system demands a dead-level, structurally solid head
